title: MLEM-FB Convergence
text:
The MLEM (Maximum Likelihood Expectation Maximisation) algorithm is the standard reconstruction method in emission tomography (PET, SPECT). It produces non-negative images and steadily improves the fit to the measured data at every iteration. But does it always find the non-negative image with highest Poisson log-likelihood? And if so, why?
This document gives a complete, self-contained proof that MLEM converges to the unique non-negative image that maximises the Poisson log-likelihood. We assume only basic calculus and probability; every mathematical step is explained.

title: MLEM-FB Convergence
text:
The MLEM (Maximum Likelihood Expectation Maximisation) algorithm is the standard reconstruction method in emission tomography (PET, SPECT). It produces non-negative images and steadily improves the fit to the measured data at every iteration. But does it always find the non-negative image with highest Poisson log-likelihood? And if so, why?
This document gives a complete, self-contained proof that MLEM converges to the unique non-negative image that maximises the Poisson log-likelihood. We assume only basic calculus and probability; every mathematical step is explained.

title: MLEM-FB Convergence
text:
2 Setup

2.1 The forward model
We measure \(I\) detector counts \(y_1, \ldots, y_I \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots\}\) (the sinogram). The image to be recovered has \(J\) voxels with (unknown) activities \(x_1, \ldots, x_J \geq 0\), collected into a column vector \(\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^J\).
The forward model relates image to expected counts:
\[\bar{y}_i(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{j=1}^{J} a_{ij}\, x_j + b_i = (A\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b})_i,
\qquad i = 1,\ldots,I,\]
where:

\(A \in \mathbb{R}^{I \times J}_{\geq 0}\) is the system matrix with entries \(a_{ij} \geq 0\). Entry \(a_{ij}\) is the probability that an event emitted in voxel \(j\) is detected in bin \(i\).
\(\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^I_{> 0}\) denotes the expectation of additive contaminations (scattered events in SPECT, and scatered and random coicidences in PET). We assume that the expectation \(\mathbf{b}\) is known (e.g. pre-estimated), and strictly positive.

We model each detector bin as an independent Poisson random variable:
\[y_i \sim \operatorname{Poisson}\!\bigl(\bar{y}_i(\mathbf{x})\bigr),
\quad \text{independently for all } i.\]


2.2 The log-likelihood
The probability of observing count \(y_i\) when the expected count is \(\bar{y}_i\) is \(P(y_i) = e^{-\bar{y}_i}\,\bar{y}_i^{y_i}/y_i!\). Taking the logarithm and summing over all bins (and dropping terms that do not depend on \(\mathbf{x}\)) gives the Poisson log-likelihood:
\[\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{I} \Bigl[y_i \log \bar{y}_i(\mathbf{x}) - \bar{y}_i(\mathbf{x})\Bigr].\]
Our goal is to find the image \(\hat{\mathbf{x}}\) that maximises \(\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x})\) subject to \(\mathbf{x} \geq \mathbf{0}\) (non-negativity).






Note\(\mathcal{L}\) is continuous and differentiable



Since we assume \(\mathbf{b} > \mathbf{0}\), \(\mathcal{L}\) is continuous and differentiable for all \(\mathbf{x} \geq \mathbf{0}\).




2.3 The MLEM update
We define the sensitivity of voxel \(j\) as the total detection probability summed over all detector bins:
\[s_j = \sum_{i=1}^{I} a_{ij} > 0, \qquad j = 1,\ldots,J.\]
Starting from an initial image \(\mathbf{x}^0 > \mathbf{0}\) (all voxels strictly positive), MLEM produces the sequence \(\mathbf{x}^0, \mathbf{x}^1, \mathbf{x}^2, \ldots\) via the update rule:
\[\boxed{x_j^{k+1} = \frac{x_j^k}{s_j} \sum_{i=1}^{I} a_{ij}\,
\frac{y_i}{\bar{y}_i(\mathbf{x}^k)}, \qquad j = 1,\ldots,J.}\]
The ratio \(y_i / \bar{y}_i(\mathbf{x}^k)\) compares measured to predicted counts in bin \(i\): it equals 1 when the current estimate is perfect, is greater than 1 when the model under-predicts, and less than 1 when it over-predicts. These correction factors are back-projected into image space and normalised by the voxel sensitivity \(s_j\).


2.4 The full-rank assumption
We assume throughout that \(A\) has full column rank, meaning no non-zero vector \(\mathbf{v} \neq \mathbf{0}\) satisfies \(A\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}\) (the nullspace of \(A\) is trivial). Assuming full column rank simplifies the convergence proof. For the more general (and much more complex) case where \(A\) does not have full column rank, see (Salvo and Defrise 2019).


2.5 Two types of constrained optima
A crucial distinction runs throughout the proof. The constrained maximum \(\hat{\mathbf{x}}\) can be of two types, distinguished by whether the unconstrained maximum of \(\mathcal{L}\) is feasible.






NoteCase (i): Interior solution — all voxels active (\(\hat{\mathbf{x}} > \mathbf{0}\))



The constrained and unconstrained maxima coincide. The gradient of \(\mathcal{L}\) vanishes at \(\hat{\mathbf{x}}\):
\[\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_j}\bigg|_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}}
= \sum_i a_{ij}\,\frac{y_i}{\bar{y}_i(\hat{\mathbf{x}})} - s_j = 0
\quad \text{for all } j.\]








NoteCase (ii): Boundary solution — some voxels are zero (some \(\hat{x}_j = 0\))



The constrained maximum lies on the boundary of the non-negative orthant: the unconstrained maximum has some negative components and is infeasible. The Karush–Kuhn–Tucker (KKT) optimality conditions hold:
\[\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_j}\bigg|_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}}
= \sum_i a_{ij}\,\frac{y_i}{\bar{y}_i(\hat{\mathbf{x}})} - s_j
\begin{cases}
= 0 & \text{if } \hat{x}_j > 0 \quad \text{(active voxel)},\\[4pt]
\leq 0 & \text{if } \hat{x}_j = 0 \quad \text{(inactive voxel)}.
\end{cases}\]
For an inactive voxel, the gradient is non-positive: increasing \(x_j\) from zero would decrease \(\mathcal{L}\), so zeroing that voxel is optimal.


Under the full-rank assumption \(\mathcal{L}\) is strictly concave (shown in Step 5), so in both cases \(\hat{\mathbf{x}}\) is unique.



2.6 Interactive visualisation of the two cases
Below is an interactive plot of the log-likelihood landscape for a simple 2-voxel problem, showing the two cases. In this example the forward model is
\[\bar{y}_1 = x_1 + b_1, \qquad \bar{y}_2 = x_1 + x_2 + b_2,\]
with \(b_1 = 0.5\) and \(b_2 = 0.5\). The plots show the log-likelihood \(\mathcal{L}(x_1, x_2)\) as a function of \(x_1\) and \(x_2\) and three different measurements: (a) \(y_1, y_2\), (b) \(y_1, y_2\), (c) \(y_1, y_2\). The blue triangle and red start show the contrained and unconstrained optima, respectively. Moreover, 20 MLEM iterations starting at \(x_1 = x_2 = 2.5\) are shown. The top row shows to examples (with and without fixed background) where the constrained optimum is in the interior, while the bottom row shows examples where the constrained optimum is on the boundary.

title: MLEM-FB Convergence
text:
3 Proof Roadmap






NoteThe five things we need to show




Bounded above: \(\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x})\) has a finite upper bound over \(\mathbf{x} \geq \mathbf{0}\).
Non-negativity preserved: \(\mathbf{x}^k \geq \mathbf{0}\) implies \(\mathbf{x}^{k+1} \geq \mathbf{0}\); and \(\mathbf{x}^0 > \mathbf{0}\) implies \(\mathbf{x}^k > \mathbf{0}\) for all \(k\).
Monotonic increase: \(\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}^{k+1}) \geq \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}^k)\), with strict inequality whenever \(\mathbf{x}^k \neq \hat{\mathbf{x}}\).
Bounded iterates: \(\{\mathbf{x}^k\}\) remains in a compact set.
Convergence: \(\mathbf{x}^k \to \hat{\mathbf{x}}\) as \(k \to \infty\).



Steps 1–3 imply that \(\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}^k)\) is monotonically increasing and bounded above, so it converges to some value \(\mathcal{L}^*\). Steps 4 and 5 show that the iterates themselves converge to \(\hat{\mathbf{x}}\).

title: MLEM-FB Convergence
section: 4 Step 1: The Log-Likelihood is Bounded Above
text:
4 Step 1: The Log-Likelihood is Bounded Above
The Poisson log-likelihood can be rewritten as:
\[\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x})
= \sum_i \bigl(y_i \log y_i - y_i\bigr)
- \underbrace{\sum_i \Bigl[y_i \log\tfrac{y_i}{\bar{y}_i} + \bar{y}_i - y_i\Bigr]}_{\geq\; 0}.\]
To see this, write \(y_i \log \bar{y}_i = y_i \log y_i - y_i\log(y_i/\bar{y}_i)\) and rearrange. The underlined term is a sum of generalised KL divergences \(d(y_i, \bar{y}_i) = y_i \log(y_i/\bar{y}_i) + \bar{y}_i - y_i \geq 0\) (each follows from \(\log u \leq u - 1\) with \(u = \bar{y}_i/y_i\)). Therefore:
\[\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}) \leq \sum_i \bigl(y_i \log y_i - y_i\bigr)
=: \mathcal{L}_{\max} < \infty. \qquad \square\]






TipConvention: \(0 \log 0 = 0\)



When \(y_i = 0\) the term \(y_i \log y_i\) is formally \(0 \cdot (-\infty)\). We adopt \(0 \log 0 := 0\), justified by \(\lim_{y\to 0^+} y\log y = 0\).

title: MLEM-FB Convergence
text:
5 Step 2: MLEM Preserves Non-Negativity
Write the update as \(x_j^{k+1} = x_j^k \cdot f_j^k\), where the update factor for voxel \(j\) is
\[f_j^k = \frac{1}{s_j}\sum_{i=1}^{I} a_{ij}\,\frac{y_i}{\bar{y}_i(\mathbf{x}^k)}.\]
Because \(a_{ij}\geq 0\), \(y_i \geq 0\), and \(\bar{y}_i(\mathbf{x}^k) > 0\), we have \(f_j^k \geq 0\). Therefore \(x_j^k \geq 0 \Rightarrow x_j^{k+1} \geq 0\), and by induction \(\mathbf{x}^k \geq \mathbf{0}\) for all \(k \geq 0\). \(\square\)
A finer dichotomy. Since \(\bar{y}_i(\mathbf{x}^k) \geq b_i > 0\), the factor \(f_j^k\) is zero if and only if \(\sum_i a_{ij} y_i = 0\). Partition the voxels once and for all into
\[\mathcal{A} = \bigl\{j : {\textstyle\sum_i} a_{ij}y_i > 0\bigr\},
\qquad
\mathcal{Z} = \bigl\{j : {\textstyle\sum_i} a_{ij}y_i = 0\bigr\}.\]

Voxels \(j\in\mathcal{Z}\): \(f_j^k = 0\) for every \(k\), so \(x_j^1 = 0\) and \(x_j^k = 0\) for all \(k\geq 1\). Furthermore, \(\partial\mathcal{L}/\partial x_j
= \sum_i a_{ij}(y_i/\bar{y}_i - 1) = -s_j < 0\) everywhere on the feasible set, so \(\hat{x}_j = 0\). MLEM correctly identifies these voxels in a single step.
Voxels \(j\in\mathcal{A}\): \(f_j^k > 0\) for all \(k\) (since \(\bar{y}_i\geq b_i>0\)). Starting from \(\mathbf{x}^0>\mathbf{0}\), these voxels satisfy \(x_j^k > 0\) for all \(k\geq 0\) by induction.

title: MLEM-FB Convergence
section: 6 Step 3: Each MLEM Update Increases the Log-Likelihood
text:
6 Step 3: Each MLEM Update Increases the Log-Likelihood
This is the most important step, using the EM framework.

6.1 The complete-data idea
Maximising \(\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x})\) directly is hard because \(\log \bar{y}_i
= \log(\sum_j a_{ij} x_j + b_i)\) couples all unknowns. The EM trick is to imagine we could also observe which voxel each detected event originated from — information the detector does not record.
Define the complete data \(n_{ij}\) as the (unobserved) number of events detected in bin \(i\) from voxel \(j\), and \(n_{i0}\) for background:
\[n_{ij} \sim \operatorname{Poisson}(a_{ij}\, x_j), \quad j \geq 1,
\qquad n_{i0} \sim \operatorname{Poisson}(b_i), \qquad \text{all independent,}\]
with \(y_i = \sum_{j=0}^{J} n_{ij}\). The complete-data log-likelihood decouples over voxels:
\[\log p(\mathbf{n} \mid \mathbf{x})
= \sum_{j=1}^{J} \left[\log x_j \underbrace{\sum_i n_{ij}}_{\text{events from }j}
- x_j \underbrace{\sum_i a_{ij}}_{s_j}\right] +  \underbrace{\left[ \sum_i n_{i0} \log b_i - b_i \right]}_{\text{const. indep. of x}}\]


6.2 The \(Q\) function
Since \(\mathbf{n}\) is not observed, EM replaces \(\log p(\mathbf{n}\mid\mathbf{x})\) by its conditional expectation given the data \(\mathbf{y}\) and current estimate \(\mathbf{x}^k\):
\[Q(\mathbf{x} \mid \mathbf{x}^k)
= E\!\left[\log p(\mathbf{n}\mid\mathbf{x}) \;\Big|\; \mathbf{y},\, \mathbf{x}^k\right].\]
Using the Poisson splitting property (if independent Poisson variables sum to \(n\), their conditional joint distribution is Multinomial with probabilities proportional to their means - see Section 10.1), one shows:
\[E[n_{ij} \mid y_i, \mathbf{x}^k]
= \frac{a_{ij}\, x_j^k}{\bar{y}_i(\mathbf{x}^k)}\, y_i =: \hat{n}_{ij}^k.\]
The observed count \(y_i\) is apportioned among voxels in proportion to their predicted contributions. Substituting:
\[Q(\mathbf{x} \mid \mathbf{x}^k)
= \sum_{j=1}^{J} \left[\hat{n}_j^k \log x_j - s_j\, x_j\right] + \text{const},
\qquad \hat{n}_j^k = \sum_i \hat{n}_{ij}^k.\]
This is separable in \(x_j\): each voxel can be updated independently.


6.3 The fundamental identity
From \(p(\mathbf{n}\mid\mathbf{x}) = p(\mathbf{y}\mid\mathbf{x})\cdot p(\mathbf{n}\mid\mathbf{y},\mathbf{x})\), taking logarithms and then the conditional expectation \(E[\,\cdot\mid\mathbf{y},\mathbf{x}^k]\):
\[Q(\mathbf{x}\mid\mathbf{x}^k)
= \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}) + H(\mathbf{x}\mid\mathbf{x}^k),\]
where \(H(\mathbf{x}\mid\mathbf{x}^k) = E[\log p(\mathbf{n}\mid\mathbf{y},\mathbf{x})\mid\mathbf{y},\mathbf{x}^k]\). Rearranging gives the fundamental identity:
\[\boxed{\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}) = Q(\mathbf{x}\mid\mathbf{x}^k) - H(\mathbf{x}\mid\mathbf{x}^k).}\]


6.4 The EM guarantee: \(\mathcal{L}\) never decreases
The M-step sets \(\mathbf{x}^{k+1} = \arg\max_{\mathbf{x}\geq\mathbf{0}} Q(\mathbf{x}\mid\mathbf{x}^k)\). Using the fundamental identity at \(\mathbf{x}^{k+1}\) and \(\mathbf{x}^k\):
\[\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}^{k+1}) - \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}^k)
= \underbrace{\bigl[Q(\mathbf{x}^{k+1}\mid\mathbf{x}^k)
  - Q(\mathbf{x}^k\mid\mathbf{x}^k)\bigr]}_{\text{Term A}\;\geq\;0}
+ \underbrace{\bigl[H(\mathbf{x}^k\mid\mathbf{x}^k)
  - H(\mathbf{x}^{k+1}\mid\mathbf{x}^k)\bigr]}_{\text{Term B}\;\geq\;0}.\]
Term A \(\geq 0\): \(\mathbf{x}^{k+1}\) maximises \(Q(\cdot\mid\mathbf{x}^k)\) by construction.
Term B \(\geq 0\): The difference equals \(D_{\mathrm{KL}}\!\bigl(p(\mathbf{n}\mid\mathbf{y},\mathbf{x}^k)\,\|\,
p(\mathbf{n}\mid\mathbf{y},\mathbf{x}^{k+1})\bigr) \geq 0\) (KL divergence is always non-negative).
Therefore \(\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}^{k+1}) \geq \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}^k)\). \(\square\)






NoteWhy the KL divergence is non-negative



\(D_{\mathrm{KL}}(p\|q) = \sum_{\mathbf{n}} p(\mathbf{n})\log\frac{p(\mathbf{n})}{q(\mathbf{n})} \geq 0\) follows from Jensen’s inequality applied to the concave function \(\log\):
\[D_{\mathrm{KL}}(p\|q)
= -\sum_{\mathbf{n}} p(\mathbf{n})\log\frac{q(\mathbf{n})}{p(\mathbf{n})}
\geq -\log\!\left(\sum_{\mathbf{n}} p(\mathbf{n})\cdot\frac{q(\mathbf{n})}{p(\mathbf{n})}\right)
= -\log 1 = 0.\]




6.5 The M-step gives the MLEM update
Maximise \(Q(\mathbf{x}\mid\mathbf{x}^k) = \sum_j [\hat{n}_j^k \log x_j - s_j x_j]\) over \(x_j \geq 0\). Each term is strictly concave in \(x_j\) with an unconstrained maximum at \(x_j = \hat{n}_j^k / s_j > 0\). Since this is already non-negative, the constrained maximum equals the unconstrained one:
\[\frac{\partial Q}{\partial x_j} = \frac{\hat{n}_j^k}{x_j} - s_j = 0
\;\Longrightarrow\;
x_j^{k+1} = \frac{\hat{n}_j^k}{s_j}
= \frac{x_j^k}{s_j} \sum_i a_{ij}\,\frac{y_i}{\bar{y}_i(\mathbf{x}^k)},\]
which is exactly the MLEM formula. \(\square\)






NoteMLEM as preconditioned gradient ascent



Rewrite \(\hat{n}_j^k = x_j^k \sum_i a_{ij}\,y_i/\bar{y}_i(\mathbf{x}^k)\) and note that the gradient of the log-likelihood is
\[\frac{\partial\mathcal{L}}{\partial x_j}(\mathbf{x}^k)
= \sum_i a_{ij}\!\left(\frac{y_i}{\bar{y}_i(\mathbf{x}^k)}-1\right)
= \frac{\hat{n}_j^k}{x_j^k} - s_j \qquad (x_j^k>0).\]
Substituting into the MLEM formula gives
\[\boxed{x_j^{k+1} = x_j^k + \frac{x_j^k}{s_j}\,
\frac{\partial\mathcal{L}}{\partial x_j}(\mathbf{x}^k).} \tag{$**$}\]
This is a gradient ascent step with a diagonal preconditioner \(P_j^k = x_j^k/s_j\). Two immediate consequences used throughout:

A-voxels (\(j\in\mathcal{A}\)): \(x_j^k>0\), so \(x_j^{k+1}=x_j^k\) if and only if \(\partial\mathcal{L}/\partial x_j(\mathbf{x}^k)=0\).
Z-voxels (\(j\in\mathcal{Z}\)): \(x_j^k=0\) for all \(k\geq 1\); the preconditioner vanishes and the voxel is permanently pinned at zero.









TipWhen does \(\mathcal{L}\) increase strictly?



The EM guarantee gives \(\geq\); for convergence we need to know when equality holds.
Term A \(= 0\) iff \(\mathbf{x}^{k+1}=\mathbf{x}^k\), i.e., every per-voxel step in \((**)\) is zero. For \(j\in\mathcal{Z}\) the step is always zero (pinned). For \(j\in\mathcal{A}\), \(x_j^k>0\), so the step is zero iff \(\partial\mathcal{L}/\partial x_j(\mathbf{x}^k)=0\).
By KKT + strict concavity of \(\mathcal{L}\), the unique point satisfying \(\partial\mathcal{L}/\partial x_j = 0\) for all \(j\in\mathcal{A}\) (together with \(x_j=0\) for all \(j\in\mathcal{Z}\)) is \(\hat{\mathbf{x}}\). Therefore \(\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}^{k+1})>\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}^k)\) whenever \(\mathbf{x}^k\neq\hat{\mathbf{x}}\), whether \(\hat{\mathbf{x}}\) is interior or on the boundary.

title: MLEM-FB Convergence
text:
7 Step 4: The Iterates Are Bounded
From Steps 1 and 3, \(\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}^k)\) is monotonically increasing. All iterates therefore lie in the super-level set:
\[\mathcal{S} = \{\mathbf{x} \geq \mathbf{0} : \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}) \geq \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}^0)\}.\]
We now show \(\mathcal{S}\) is bounded (and hence compact, since it is also closed).
Why \(\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}) \to -\infty\) as \(\|\mathbf{x}\| \to \infty\): \(\mathcal{L}\) contains the term \(-\sum_i \bar{y}_i(\mathbf{x}) = -\mathbf{1}^\top A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{1}^\top\mathbf{b}\). When \(\mathbf{x} \geq \mathbf{0}\) and \(\|\mathbf{x}\| \to \infty\), at least one \(x_j \to \infty\). Since \(A\) has full column rank, \(\sum_i a_{ij} = s_j > 0\), so \(s_j x_j \to \infty\), driving \(\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}) \to -\infty\).
Since \(\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}) \to -\infty\) outside any ball but \(\mathcal{S}\) requires \(\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}) \geq \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}^0) > -\infty\), the set \(\mathcal{S}\) must be bounded. All iterates \(\mathbf{x}^k\) lie in \(\mathcal{S}\). \(\square\)

title: MLEM-FB Convergence
section: 8 Step 5: Convergence to the Constrained ML Solution
text:
8 Step 5: Convergence to the Constrained ML Solution
We now show \(\mathbf{x}^k \to \hat{\mathbf{x}} = \arg\max_{\mathbf{x}\geq\mathbf{0}}\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x})\).

8.1 Strict concavity and uniqueness of \(\hat{\mathbf{x}}\)
The Hessian of \(\mathcal{L}\) is:
\[\nabla^2\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x})
= -A^\top D(\mathbf{x})\,A, \qquad
D(\mathbf{x}) = \operatorname{diag}\!\left(\frac{y_i}{\bar{y}_i(\mathbf{x})^2}\right) > 0.\]
Because \(A\) has full column rank, \(A^\top D A\) is positive definite for any diagonal \(D > 0\), so \(\nabla^2\mathcal{L}\) is negative definite: \(\mathcal{L}\) is strictly concave. A strictly concave function over a convex set has at most one maximum, so \(\hat{\mathbf{x}}\) is unique.


8.2 \(\hat{\mathbf{x}}\) is a fixed point of MLEM
We show \(F(\hat{\mathbf{x}}) = \hat{\mathbf{x}}\), where \(F\) denotes one MLEM step.
Active voxels (\(\hat{x}_j > 0\)): The KKT condition gives \(\partial\mathcal{L}/\partial x_j\big|_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}} = 0\), so the gradient ascent step \((**)\) is zero: \(F(\hat{\mathbf{x}})_j = \hat{x}_j\). \(\checkmark\)
Inactive voxels (\(\hat{x}_j = 0\)): The multiplicative structure gives \(F(\hat{\mathbf{x}})_j = 0 \times (\ldots) = 0 = \hat{x}_j\), regardless of the gradient — MLEM cannot create activity from nothing. \(\checkmark\)


8.3 The log-likelihood values converge
From Steps 1 and 3: \(\{\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}^k)\}\) is monotonically increasing and bounded above. By the monotone convergence theorem it converges:
\[\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}^k) \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{L}^* \;\leq\; \mathcal{L}(\hat{\mathbf{x}}).\]
We will show \(\mathcal{L}^* = \mathcal{L}(\hat{\mathbf{x}})\).


8.4 A convergent subsequence exists
Since all iterates lie in the compact set \(\mathcal{S}\), the Bolzano–Weierstrass theorem (every bounded sequence in \(\mathbb{R}^J\) has a convergent subsequence) guarantees a subsequence:
\[\mathbf{x}^{k_m} \;\longrightarrow\; \mathbf{x}^* \in \mathcal{S}.\]
Because \(\mathbf{x}^{k_m}\) is a subsequence of \(\{\mathbf{x}^k\}\) and \(\mathcal{L}\) is continuous:
\[\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}^*) = \lim_{m\to\infty}\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}^{k_m}) = \mathcal{L}^*.\]
(Two facts combine: a subsequence of a convergent sequence shares its limit; \(\mathcal{L}\) is continuous so the limit commutes with the function.)


8.5 Every accumulation point equals \(\hat{\mathbf{x}}\)
Because \(\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}^k)\) is non-decreasing and bounded above (Steps 1 & 3), it converges and its increments vanish:
\[\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}^{k+1}) - \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}^k) \;\longrightarrow\; 0.\]
For the subsequence \(\mathbf{x}^{k_m}\to\mathbf{x}^*\), continuity of \(F\) and \(\mathcal{L}\) then gives
\[\mathcal{L}(F(\mathbf{x}^*)) - \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}^*) =
\lim_{m\to\infty}\bigl[\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}^{k_m+1})
- \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}^{k_m})\bigr] = 0. \tag{$*$}\]
We verify the KKT conditions at \(\mathbf{x}^*\) voxel by voxel, using the gradient ascent form \((**)\).

\(j\in\mathcal{Z}\): \(x_j^*=0\) and \(\partial\mathcal{L}/\partial x_j =
-s_j<0\) everywhere on the feasible set. KKT satisfied. \(\checkmark\)
\(j\in\mathcal{A},\; x_j^*>0\): From \((*)\), \(\mathcal{L}(F(\mathbf{x}^*))=\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}^*)\). By Step 3, MLEM strictly increases \(\mathcal{L}\) unless at a fixed point, so \(F(\mathbf{x}^*)=\mathbf{x}^*\). Since \(x_j^*>0\), the gradient ascent step \((**)\) is zero: \(\partial\mathcal{L}/\partial x_j\big|_{\mathbf{x}^*}=0\). KKT satisfied. \(\checkmark\)
\(j\in\mathcal{A},\; x_j^*=0\): Since \(x_j^{k_m}>0\) for all \(m\) (Step 2) and \(x_j^{k_m}\to 0\), the MLEM update gives
\[x_j^{k_m+1} = x_j^{k_m}\cdot\frac{1}{s_j}\sum_i
a_{ij}\frac{y_i}{\bar{y}_i(\mathbf{x}^{k_m})}.\]
If \(\partial\mathcal{L}/\partial x_j\big|_{\mathbf{x}^*}>0\), the ratio exceeds \(1\) near \(\mathbf{x}^*\), so \(x_j^{k_m+1}>x_j^{k_m}\) for large \(m\) — incompatible with \(x_j^{k_m}\to 0\). Therefore \(\partial\mathcal{L}/\partial x_j\big|_{\mathbf{x}^*}\leq 0\). KKT satisfied. \(\checkmark\)







Note



Bullets 1 and 3 together show that an accumulation point can only have a zero component where \(\hat{\mathbf{x}}\) is also zero: if \(x_j^*=0\), the KKT condition \(\partial\mathcal{L}/\partial x_j\leq 0\) holds at \(\mathbf{x}^*\), which — together with the full KKT structure — forces \(\mathbf{x}^*=\hat{\mathbf{x}}\) and hence \(\hat{x}_j=0\). In particular, \(\mathbf{x}^*=\mathbf{0}\) is only consistent with the KKT conditions if \(\hat{\mathbf{x}}=\mathbf{0}\), i.e. all voxels are in \(\mathcal{Z}\) and no counts are observed anywhere.


Since \(\mathcal{L}\) is strictly concave, the KKT conditions are necessary and sufficient for the unique constrained maximum, so \(\mathbf{x}^*=\hat{\mathbf{x}}\), and therefore \(\mathcal{L}^*=\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}^*)=\mathcal{L}(\hat{\mathbf{x}})\). \(\square\)


8.6 The full sequence converges
Every convergent subsequence of \(\{\mathbf{x}^k\}\) shares the same limit \(\hat{\mathbf{x}}\) by the argument above: any accumulation point achieves \(\mathcal{L}^* = \mathcal{L}(\hat{\mathbf{x}})\), and strict concavity forces it to equal \(\hat{\mathbf{x}}\). Since the sequence is bounded (Step 4), this single accumulation point forces the full sequence to converge:
\[\mathbf{x}^k \;\longrightarrow\; \hat{\mathbf{x}} \quad \text{as } k \to \infty.
\qquad \square\]

title: MLEM-FB Convergence
text:
9 Summary






NoteConvergence theorem



Let \(y_i \sim \operatorname{Poisson}((A\mathbf{x}+\mathbf{b})_i)\) with \(A\) having full column rank and \(b_i > 0\). Starting from any \(\mathbf{x}^0 > \mathbf{0}\), the MLEM iterates \(\mathbf{x}^k\) converge to the unique constrained maximum \(\hat{\mathbf{x}}\) of the Poisson log-likelihood over \(\mathbf{x} \geq \mathbf{0}\).










Step
What we showed
Key tool




1
\(\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x})\) bounded above by \(\mathcal{L}_{\max}\)
\(\log u \leq u-1\) (generalised KL)


2
\(\mathbf{x}^k > \mathbf{0}\) for all \(k\) (from \(\mathbf{x}^0 > \mathbf{0}\))
Multiplicative structure of MLEM


3
\(\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}^{k+1}) > \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}^k)\) for \(\mathbf{x}^k \neq \hat{\mathbf{x}}\)
EM framework (\(Q\) function + KL \(\geq 0\))


4
\(\{\mathbf{x}^k\} \subset \mathcal{S}\) (compact)
Full rank \(\Rightarrow\) coercivity of \(\mathcal{L}\)


5
\(\mathbf{x}^k \to \hat{\mathbf{x}}\)
Bolzano–Weierstrass + strict concavity + KKT



Step 3 is the heart of the proof: the EM framework guarantees progress at every step, with the KL divergence as the reason. Steps 4 and 5 ensure this progress is not wasted — the sequence stays bounded and the accumulated improvements converge to the correct answer, whether \(\hat{\mathbf{x}}\) lies in the interior of \(\mathbf{x} \geq \mathbf{0}\) (all voxels active) or on its boundary (some voxels zeroed out).

title: MLEM-FB Convergence
text:
10 Appendix

10.1 The conditional expectation via Poisson splitting
Setup. In the complete-data model each voxel \(j\) contributes independently to detector bin \(i\): \(n_{ij} \sim \operatorname{Poisson}(a_{ij}x_j)\), and the observed count is the total \(y_i = \sum_j n_{ij}\).
Poisson splitting property. Let \(N_1, \ldots, N_J\) be independent Poisson random variables with means \(\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_J\), and let \(N = N_1 + \cdots + N_J \sim \operatorname{Poisson}(\lambda)\), \(\lambda = \sum_j \lambda_j\). Then the conditional distribution of \((N_1, \ldots, N_J)\) given \(N = n\) is multinomial:
\[(N_1, \ldots, N_J) \mid N = n \;\sim\;
\operatorname{Multinomial}\!\left(n;\;
\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda}, \ldots, \frac{\lambda_J}{\lambda}\right).\]

Proof. The joint probability of \((N_1 = n_1, \ldots, N_J = n_J)\) with \(\sum_j n_j = n\) factors as a product of Poisson probabilities:
\[P(N_1 = n_1, \ldots, N_J = n_J)
= \prod_j \frac{e^{-\lambda_j} \lambda_j^{n_j}}{n_j!}
= e^{-\lambda} \frac{\prod_j \lambda_j^{n_j}}{\prod_j n_j!}.\]
Dividing by \(P(N = n) = e^{-\lambda}\lambda^n / n!\) gives:
\[P(N_1 = n_1, \ldots, N_J = n_J \mid N = n)
= \frac{n!}{\prod_j n_j!} \prod_j \left(\frac{\lambda_j}{\lambda}\right)^{n_j},\]
which is the \(\operatorname{Multinomial}(n;\, \lambda_1/\lambda, \ldots, \lambda_J/\lambda)\) probability mass function.

Application. At iteration \(k\), set \(\lambda_j = a_{ij}x_j^k\) as the predicted Poisson mean for the contribution of voxel \(j\) to bin \(i\). By the splitting property, conditioned on the observed count \(y_i\):
\[(n_{i1}, \ldots, n_{iJ}) \mid y_i,\, \mathbf{x}^k
\;\sim\;
\operatorname{Multinomial}\!\left(y_i;\;
\frac{a_{i1}x_1^k}{\bar{y}_i(\mathbf{x}^k)},
\ldots,
\frac{a_{iJ}x_J^k}{\bar{y}_i(\mathbf{x}^k)}\right),\]
where \(\bar{y}_i(\mathbf{x}^k) = \sum_j a_{ij}x_j^k + b_i\). Using the fact that \(E[N_j \mid N = n] = n \cdot p_j\) for a multinomial:
\[\boxed{\hat{n}_{ij}^k \;:=\; E[n_{ij} \mid y_i,\, \mathbf{x}^k]
\;=\; \frac{a_{ij}\,x_j^k}{\bar{y}_i(\mathbf{x}^k)}\,y_i.}\]






NoteInterpretation



The formula apportions the observed count \(y_i\) among voxels in proportion to their predicted contributions \(a_{ij}x_j^k\). If voxel \(j\) is predicted to account for 30 % of the expected signal in bin \(i\), it is credited with 30 % of the observed photons — a probabilistic bookkeeping step that underpins every EM-type reconstruction algorithm.








TipBackground term \(b_i\)



If the background (scatter, randoms) is modelled as an additional independent Poisson source with known mean \(b_i\), it simply adds one more component to the multinomial split. Because \(b_i\) is a known constant rather than an unknown to be estimated, its component does not appear in \(Q\), and the formula above for the image voxels is unchanged.
